segments.s529-g57: "به" marks direction ('to'); "کردان" is a plural noun here ('the doers/agents'); "فرستاده" is a noun 'messenger/sent one' and is the head of the noun phrase; "کارآگهان" is postpositive and qualifies فرستاده ('investigators/those aware of the matter'). The whole phrase therefore means 'to the doers, messengers who are investigators' (i.e., he sent investigators to those doers).
segments.s529-g58: "کجا" (where) fronted as an indirect interrogative/relative; "کارِ ایشان" = 'their affair/deed' (noun + possessive); "بجوید" is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of 'seek/search' used here as purpose/result (’to seek/find out’); "نهان" (placed sentence‑finally) describes the hidden nature of that work ('hidden' or 'secret'). Together: 'to seek where their hidden work is.'
segments.s529-g59: "برفتند" is a past plural verb 'they departed/went away'; "پویان" is a participial/adjectival word 'seeking/active' describing their purpose or manner ('they went off in quest'); the conjunction "و" links the clauses; "بازآمدند" is past plural 'they came back/returned.' The pair expresses: they went off in search and then returned.
segments.s529-g60: "بر" here means 'toward/to' (direction toward a person); "شاه ایران" = 'the King of Iran' (noun phrase); "فراز آمدند" is a fixed-like phrase meaning 'came up before/approached' (فراز 'on high/presence' + آمدند 'they came'). Together: 'they came up to/approached the King of Iran.'
segments.s529-g61: "که" introduces a clause of explanation/reason ('for/since'); "ایشان" = 'they'; "همه" = 'all/everyone'; "نام‌جویند" is present plural 'seek name' = 'seek fame/reputation'; "و شاد" adds 'and (are) happy' (شاد is an adjectival predicate). The clause says they are all fame‑seekers (and cheerful/merry).
segments.s529-g62: "ندارد" is present negative 'has not/does not have' (3rd singular); "کسی" = 'anyone' (subject in negative context); "بر دل" = 'upon/ in the heart' (location of sentiment); "از شاه یاد" = 'any remembrance of the king' (از introduces the object of remembrance). The literal order is 'no one has upon the heart any remembrance of the king.'
segments.s529-g63: "برآنند" is a fixed idiom 'they are intent/bent on' (they desire or plan); "کاندر" = 'in which/therein' (classical fused form of 'که در'); "صطخر اردشیر" is a place‑name plus personal name ('in Istakhr, Ardashir'). The segment expresses the object/location of their intent: what they wish to happen in Istakhr regarding Ardashir.
segments.s529-g64: "کهن گشت" = 'became old' (کهن 'old' + past گشت 'became'); "و شد" = 'and became' (reiterating/result clause); "بخت برناش پیر" breaks down as 'بخت' (fortune/fate) + "برناش" (his youthfulness/young‑state, here adjectival/possessive) + "پیر" (old). The sense: his youthful fortune turned old — i.e., his vigor/fortune was aged. Grammatically, two parallel perfective verbs describe change of state.
segments.s529-g65: چو is an archaic conjunction meaning "when" here. بشنید is a past indicative (he heard) with the subject شاه (the king) following it in the classical narrative word order (verb before subject is common in poetry). این سخن is the direct object phrase "this speech/these words," and شاد شد is a predicative clause where شد is the past-tense copula/verb meaning "became," so the whole clause reads "When the king heard these words, he became glad."
segments.s529-g66: گذشته سخن is an anaphoric phrase "the foregoing/previous speech/words." بر دلش is a prepositional phrase "upon his heart" (بر + دل + pronominal clitic ش = "his"). باد شد is a concise classical expression meaning that the words took effect in his heart or delighted him; شد is again the past copula/verb "became/was, took place," and باد here functions idiomatically (not literal "wind").
segments.s529-g67: گزین کرد is a verbal compound where گزین (root meaning "choose/select") + کرد (past-tense auxiliary "did/made") together give the past action "he chose." ازان is the old contracted form of از آن meaning "from that/from it." لشکر نامدار is a noun + postpositive adjective (common in classical Persian) meaning "renowned army/host."
segments.s529-g68: سواران (horsemen/riders) is the noun being quantified and described; شمشیرزن is a postpositive attributive noun/adjective meaning "swordsman" (i.e. riders who fight with the sword). The number phrase سی هزار ("thirty thousand") follows the noun phrase in this poetic ordering to give the quantity.
segments.s529-g69: کماندار is the noun "archer(s)." با introduces the instruments/attendants "with" (here "with arrows and quivers"). تیر is "arrow." و is the coordinating conjunction "and." ترک + ش here together form ترکش (classical orthography split), meaning "quiver," but the lemma given is ترک (which has different senses); the orthographic clitic ش is the final letter of ترکش. In context the two tokens combine as the word "ترکش" (quiver), so the phrase reads "archers with arrows and quivers (a thousand)."
segments.s529-g70: بیاورد is a past-tense verb meaning "he brought/caused to come." با خویشتن is a fixed phrase "with himself/along with him." شهریار here names or addresses the ruler ("the sovereign/king") and can function as an appositive/vocative; together the clause means "he brought with him a thousand archers, O sovereign."
segments.s529-g9: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' introducing an instance; لشکری is the noun 'army/troops' and is the object of کرد, where کرد is the simple past 'made/raised' (lit. 'did an army'). بد پارسی here is an adjectival phrase modifying لشکری ('of Persian sort/character'); in classical style the adjective follows the noun phrase it qualifies.
segments.s529-g10: فزونتر is the comparative 'more/more numerous'; ز introduces the standard of comparison ('than'); گردان او = 'his/its division/company' (گردان + possessive او). یک به سی is a numeric ratio 'one to thirty' (به = 'to' in the ratio). The whole segment states the army's superiority in numbers relative to a single enemy company.
segments.s529-g11: یکی روز literally 'one day' (a time phrase) followed by تا شب 'until night' gives the time span 'from morning/day until night'. برآویختند is the 3rd-person plural past of the classical verb برآویختن meaning 'to spring upon; to rush at' — here it is the main action ('they sallied forth/attacked').
segments.s529-g12: سپاه جهاندار is a nominal phrase 'the army of the Jehāndār' (جهاندار an honorific for the ruler/commander). بگریختند is the past plural prefixed with ب- (classical preformative) meaning 'they fled/ran away' — subject (سپاه جهاندار) + verb order.
segments.s529-g13: ز بس here means 'because of so much / owing to the great number' (ز + بس giving a causal nuance). کشته و خسته is a paired description 'killed and weary/exhausted' referring to combatants; بر دشت specifies location 'upon the plain'. Together this gives the cause clause for what follows.
segments.s529-g14: شد is the past of شدن 'became' (copulative/resultative). آوردگه را is the direct object/topic marked with را; آوردگه 'place of bringing/arrival' is used poetically for the scene/venue (here the battlefield). همه جای تنگ 'every place narrow' = 'everywhere was cramped/close.' The clause states the result: the battlefield everywhere grew cramped.
segments.s529-g15: جز از = 'except for' (جز followed by از + noun). شاه is 'the king'; با + خوارمایه سپاه = 'with / accompanied by the lowly/men of mean rank' (خوارمایه = low-born/despicable in origin, modifying سپاه). The phrase lists the only parties present apart from whom the following negative applies.
segments.s529-g16: نبد is the classical negative/existential 'there was not / did not exist' (archaic form of نبود). نامداران refers to 'the famed/renowned ones' (i.e. famous warriors). ب + دان here appear as the fused particle/compound (بدان = 'in that' / 'at that'); رزمگاه is 'battlefield'. The clause thus states that famed warriors were not to be found at that battlefield.
segments.s529-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past 3rd-person form 'he heard'. زان = 'of/from that' (referring to the news just mentioned). سرشبان اردشیر is a noun phrase 'Ardashir's chief groom' (head noun سرشبان followed by the possessive name اردشیر). The whole segment means 'when Ardashir's head-groom heard that...'.
segments.s529-g50: ببرد is a classical/persian past-form of بردن meaning 'he took/led away'. از marks origin 'from'. رمه = 'the flock/herd'. راهبر is an agent noun 'guide/leader'. چند پیر = 'a few elders' (چند = several/a few; پیر = old). Word order is typical: verb (ببرد) followed by source (از رمه) and the persons taken (راهبر چند پیر).
segments.s529-g51: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a general). ز کوه اندر آمد = 'came down/from the mountain' (ز = 'from', کوه = 'mountain', اندر آمد = 'entered/came down'). بده here is written as one token but in context functions as the locational phrase 'به ده' = 'to the village' (a poetic contraction), so the whole segment reads 'the spahbed came down from the mountain to the village.'
segments.s529-g52: ازان ده = 'from that village' (ازان = 'from that', ده = 'village'). سبک is used adjectivally 'light' (= quick, nimble). پیش او رفت = 'went before him' (پیش = 'in front of/before', او = 'him', رفت = 'went'). مه is an archaic/postpositive intensifying or honorific word here ('the great' / notable), placed after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s529-g53: سواران فرستاد = 'he sent horsemen' (سواران = 'riders/horsemen'; فرستاد = past 'sent' with an implied agent 'he'). برنا و پیر are coordinated adjectives 'young and old' modifying the riders; Persian often places such modifiers after the verb or noun they qualify in poetry.
segments.s529-g54: ازان شهر = 'from that city' (ازان = 'from that', شهر = 'city'). تا here means 'as far as' (extent marker). خوره اردشیر is a place-name (Khoreh Ardashir); خوره in the lexical senses given does not match a common noun here, so it functions as part of the toponym.
segments.s529-g55: سپه را uses را as a topicalizing/definite marker: 'the army'. چو = 'when'. آگاهی آمد = 'news/knowledge came' (literally 'awareness came' = 'they received word'). ز شاه = 'from the king'. The clause structure is temporal: 'When news came to the army from the king...'.
segments.s529-g56: همه شاددل = 'all, cheerful of heart' (همه = everyone/all; شاددل = joyful). برگرفتند is past 3rd plural 'they lifted/took up' (here 'took up [their] way' = set out). راه = 'road/way' is the object. Together: 'all, joyful, took up the road' (they set out).
segments.s529-g1: سپاهی ز اصطخر is a noun phrase 'a troop/force from Istakhr' with the poetic preposition ز = 'from' following the noun. بی‌مر is an uncertain/archaic token here (likely a proper name or epithet); its role is as a following noun or modifier. ببرد is a past/verb-form of برد 'take/carry' (here best read as 'took' or 'led'), third‑person singular; word order places the source phrase first, then the object/name, then the verb, as is common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s529-g2: بشد ساخته is a verbal complex: بشد (be + شد) here functions as a perfective/auxiliary meaning 'became' or 'was made', with ساخته (past participle 'made/prepared') giving the result 'was made ready'. تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that', کند is subjunctive/present 3rd‑person of کردن used as an auxiliary 'may do / might make', and رزم کرد is the verbal phrase 'to do battle' (رزم as noun 'battle' plus کرد 'do'); together: 'was made ready so that it might do battle.'
segments.s529-g3: به نیکی is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'by/with goodness'; ز یزدان 'from God' uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from'. همی is the old continuous/progressive particle 'still/continually' marking ongoing action; جست is the verb 'sought' (here in the sense 'seek/want'), and مزد is the object 'reward/wage'. Word order: manner/source + progressive verb + object.
segments.s529-g4: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/so that'. ریزد is a present/subjunctive‑reading of ریختن 'to pour/fall' (here 'may fall/pour down'). بر آن بوم = 'upon that soil/homeland' (بر + demonstrative آن + بوم), و connects the second parallel object, بر خون دزد = 'upon the thief's blood' (خون + noun appositive دزد); the clause expresses the hoped-for effect.
segments.s529-g5: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شاه اردشیر is the nominative 'King Ardashir' (proper name). اندرآمد is the compound verb 'entered/coming in' (اندر + آمد) used here in the past; به تنگ is a prepositional phrase 'into the narrow place/defile' with به = 'into' and تنگ used as place noun 'narrow (place)/pass'.
segments.s529-g6: پذیره is a noun 'reception/welcoming'. شدش is شد + enclitic pronoun ش/ـش 'to him' or 'upon him' and here conveys 'he was received' or 'it befell him (to be received)'. کرد is the verb 'made/did' that links to the following phrase; بی‌مر (uncertain/archaic) appears here as a noun/name and is the logical patient/agent being sent, and به جنگ 'to war' is the resulting direction. The sequence is best read as passive/causative: 'he was received and Bi‑mar was sent to war.'
segments.s529-g7: یکی here functions as 'a certain / one' (indefinite). کار 'task/deed' is the head noun; بدخوار is an epithet 'bad‑eating' (literally 'glutton' or an epithet applied to a man) modifying کار or indicating whose deed it is; دشوار گشت is the simple past 'became difficult' (دشوار = difficult; گشت = became). The phrase means that a particular act associated with the glutton turned out hard/difficult.
segments.s529-g8: ابا کرد is a verbal phrase 'he refused / was reluctant' (ابا as verb 'to refuse' plus کرد as light verb). کشور همه یار گشت is a clause 'the whole country became allies/friends' — کشور = country/realm, همه = all/everyone, یار گشت = 'became allies' (یار here = companion/ally and گشت = became). Word order: verb (refusal) then result clause describing the realm's response.
segments.s529-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the cause; خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective sequence ('the shining sun'). وز here is a noun 'gust/blast' (not a conjunction) that takes گرد و خاک ('dust') as its complement; گرد و خاک is a fixed compound 'dust and dirt'. Word order (preposition + noun phrase, then noun + complement) follows classical Persian patterns.
segments.s529-g18: زبانها is the subject 'tongues' (i.e. mouths); شد is the past of شدن 'became' (copular/become). از marks cause ('from/because of'), تشنگی is the cause 'thirst', and چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective/adverb meaning 'split/cracked' used predicatively. The clause therefore says the tongues became cracked as a result of thirst.
segments.s529-g19: هم‌انگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment'. درفشی functions as the banner/standard (object), and برآورد is a past verbal form 'brought up/raised' (classical usage can mean 'produced/raised'); شب 'night' is the agent/subject placed after the verb, a permissible order in older Persian.
segments.s529-g20: که introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'). بنشاند is a classical form meaning 'may seat/settle/put to rest' (here subjunctive/cause: 'so that it might calm'), آن is the demonstrative 'that', and جنگ و جوش و جلب are three nouns in a row ('battle / surge / commotion') listing what is to be quieted.
segments.s529-g21: یکی here means 'someone / a certain one' (indefinite subject). آتشی is an indefinite object 'a fire' (adjectival ending -ی used as noun), دید is the simple past 'saw'. The prepositional sequence بر سوی کوه ('toward the mountain') uses two directional particles for emphasis; classical Persian often stacks such particles.
segments.s529-g22: بیامد is the past 'came'. جهاندار (sovereign/conqueror) is the subject; با آن گروه is a prepositional phrase 'with that band/company' modifying the verb. The verb–subject order (verb before its noun) is common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s529-g23: سوی آتش 'toward the fire' is the directional prepositional phrase. آورد is the past of آوردن 'brought/turned' and combines with روی 'face' to mean 'turned his face'. The isolated ا before ردشیر is the vocative/exclamatory particle 'O' (classical usage) introducing the name ردشیر.
segments.s529-g24: همان اندکی مرد is a noun phrase 'those same few men' (همان demonstrative, اندکی 'a few', مرد 'men'). Adjectives برنا و پیر follow the noun in classical Persian and here mean 'young and old' (i.e., both the youthful and the elderly among that small group).
segments.s529-g41: بپرسید is the literary past 3rd‑person singular 'he asked' (verb before object, typical in older Persian). زان is a demonstrative 'that/from that' linking back to the person just mentioned; سرشبان راه شاه is a noun phrase in genitive order ('head groom of the king's road') with راه شاه = 'royal road'.
segments.s529-g42: کز here introduces an indirect wh‑question with the sense 'from which / from where'; ایدر = 'here'; کجا = 'where'; یابم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/optative form used in reported questions ('may I find / can I find'); آرامگاه = 'tomb' is the object of یابم. Word order is typical: kinematic question phrase (کز ایدر کجا) + subordinate verb (یابم).
segments.s529-g43: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed word order in narrative—چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'an answer' (object). The conjunction که introduces the reported content that follows. آباد جای = 'an inhabited/settled place' (adjective آباد before noun جای).
segments.s529-g44: نیابی is the negative (in form here treated as the verb 'find' in this clause) meaning 'you will not find' in literary usage (negative sense is carried by the prefix ن‑). مگر = 'unless / except'; باشدت is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person subjunctive/optative construction literally 'may there be to you' (i.e. 'you have'); رهنمای = 'a guide'. Together the clause means 'you will not find (an inhabited place), unless you have a guide.'
segments.s529-g45: از ایدر = 'from here' (از + demonstrative). کنون = 'now/presently' modifies the following nominal clause. چار فرسنگ راه is a compact measure phrase 'a road of four farsangs' (number + unit + noun); the copula or verb 'is' is omitted as is common in Persian nominal statements.
segments.s529-g46: چو رفتی is a temporal clause 'when/if you go' (چو = 'when'); رفتی is 2nd‑person perfect/past form used in temporal protasis to refer to a future action. پدید آید is a two‑word predicate where پدید 'visible / come into view' combines with آید 'comes' (3rd person) to mean 'will appear / come into view'; آرامگاه is the subject/what will appear ('the tomb').
segments.s529-g47: وزان = 'and from that / from that time' linking back to the previous clause. روی here functions as a nominalized notion of movement or appearance (literary 'the going/face/appearance'), and پیوسته شد is the compound verbal phrase 'became continuous / continued' (پیوسته 'continuously' + شد 'became/was'). ده به ده is a repeated phrase 'village to village' indicating successive places; word order places the motion/state first and the route phrase after it.
segments.s529-g48: به ده = 'to/toward the village' (به = to/towards + ده = village). در یکی = 'into one / in one' (در = in/into; یکی = one/a certain one). نامبردار مه is a noun phrase 'renowned (نامبردار) Mah (مه)' where مه is a proper/name or epithet (here functioning as the place‑name or epithet). Grammatically the segment specifies which particular village/place they entered—'one called the famed Mah.'
segments.s529-g33: ز + noun-phrase marks origin or source: ز خفتان = 'from slumber' (poetic instead of از خواب). شایسته is an adjective/adverb modifying خفتان ("befitting/appropriate"). بد precedes بسترش and qualifies it ("ill/poor" or "unfitting"), and بسترش is the possessed noun 'his bedding/bed'. This whole chunk is a pre-verbal noun phrase (the object/thing put) that awaits the verb in the next segment.
segments.s529-g34: به بالین is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the pillow'; نهاد is the simple past of نهادن 'placed/put'. آن کیی is the subject 'that Kayi (the Kayanian king)' and مغفرش is the possessed noun 'his maghfer/covering'. Word order is verb (نهاد) followed by subject and object; the verb here completes the action signalled in the previous segment.
segments.s529-g35: سپیده 'dawn' is the subject; چو here means 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'appeared/rose' (poetic usage). ز دریای آب is a poetic source phrase 'from the sea of water' describing where the dawn comes from. The clause is temporal, setting the time for the following action.
segments.s529-g36: سر شاه ایران is an appositive/title phrase: سر here means 'chief/head' = the king (an epithet for the ruler). برآمد is the simple past 'rose/arose' and ز خواب is the source 'from sleep'. The verb is in the past tense and the whole clause states the Shah's waking as the result/time set by the previous clause.
segments.s529-g37: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. به بالین او is the locative prepositional phrase 'to/at his pillow/bedside' and سرشبان is the agent 'the head-groom' (the noun follows the verb here, a common poetic order: verb + locative + subject).
segments.s529-g38: که introduces the reported blessing/clause. پدرام is a personal name. باد is the optative/formulaic particle 'may it be' (a wish or benediction). از تو here functions roughly 'for you'/'on your behalf' (the preposition sense 'of/about' used idiomatically), تو is 'you', روز و شبان is the time phrase 'day and night' (classical poetic plural). The whole is a short wish uttered by the groom.
segments.s529-g39: چه آمد که is the fixed idiom 'what happened that...' or 'how came it that...'; آمد is used in the sense 'happened/occurred'. این جای راه تو بود = 'this place was your way/path' (این جای = this spot/place, راه تو = your road/route, بود is the past copula 'was'). The clause questions why the king should be using this place.
segments.s529-g40: که here introduces the explanatory clause 'that...'. در خور is the compound expressing suitability/'worthy of' (here split into preposition در + the word خور); خوابگاه تو 'your sleeping-quarters' is the noun phrase and بود is the past copula 'was'. Grammatically this clause explains that the place was not fit as the king's lodging.
segments.s529-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when...'. تنگ اندر آمد is an intransitive verbal phrase meaning 'became close / drew tight' (تنگ + اندر + آمد), with اندر marking the inward/state sense; شبانان is the subject 'the shepherds' and بدید is the past tense verb 'saw'. Word order is poetic but functions as 'When X happened, the shepherds saw.'
segments.s529-g26: بران is a discourse adverb 'thereupon / on that' that links to the previous clause. میش و بز are the objects 'ewes and goats', پاسبانان is the (agent) plural 'the guards/herdsmen' and بدید is again the past 'saw'. Poetic word order places objects before the agent and verb but the clause reads 'thereupon the guards saw the ewes and goats.'
segments.s529-g27: فرود آمد is a compound past meaning 'descended / alighted' (فرود + آمد). از marks source 'from', here taking باره as 'baggage/retinue/pack' or 'litter/entourage' connected to شاه و سپاه ('the king and the army') — so 'from the king's retinue and the army.' Word order is 'verb + از + noun-phrase'.
segments.s529-g28: دهانش is دهان + enclitic possessive -ش 'his mouth'. پر از is the predicate 'full of', with از introducing the material (خاک = earth/dust). آوردگاه is a compound meaning 'battlefield' (place of bringing/encounter). The whole is a verbless predication: 'his mouth (was) full of the battlefield's dust.'
segments.s529-g29: از ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from them'. سبک here is used adjectivally/adverbially (an epithet or 'light/brief') modifying the action or the subject; اردشیر is the subject name, and آب خواست is the simple past compound 'asked for water' (آب + خواست). The clause reads 'Ardashir asked them for water.'
segments.s529-g30: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb 'immediately / at that moment'. ببردند is past plural 'they took / carried (away)'. با introduces accompaniment/instrument 'with', and آب ماست here is a fixed collocation meaning the liquid of yogurt (whey) offered as a moistening/drink; note the printed segmentation separates ما and ست, but in context ماست is the noun 'yogurt/whey'. The clause means 'at once they carried (him) with water and whey.'
segments.s529-g31: بیاسود is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'not at rest / uneasy' (بی- negative + آسود). و coordinates the next action. لختی is an adverb 'for a little while'. چرید is past 'grazed / foraged' (used literally for animals or metaphorically for picking/feeding), followed by آنچ دید 'what he saw' (آنچ = 'that which / whatever' + دید 'saw'), so the clause reads 'he did not rest, and for a short while grazed on what he saw.'
segments.s529-g32: شب تیره is a noun phrase 'the dark night'. خفتان here functions as 'the sleepers / sleeping (people)' or a nominal related to sleep, followed by the compound verbal construction به سر بر کشید (به سر + بر + کشید) where بر + کشیدن 'to raise up' yields 'raised its head' — a poetic way to say 'night fell / darkness lifted its head over the sleepers.' Note: the supplied lemma senses for خفتان and کشید don't list this exact use, so those are irregular/archaic here.

sentences.s529-s29: He sent investigators to the doers to find out where their secret work lay.
sentences.s529-s30: They set out searching and then returned; they approached the King of Iran.
sentences.s529-s31: For they all seek fame, and no one has in his heart any remembrance of the king.
sentences.s529-s32: They are intent that in Istakhr Ardashir should grow old, and that his youthful fortune become aged.
sentences.s529-s33: When the king heard these words he grew glad; the foregoing speech took hold of his heart.
sentences.s529-s34: He chose from that renowned host thirty thousand horsemen, skilled with the sword.
sentences.s529-s35: He brought with him a thousand archers, with bows and quivers, O sovereign.
sentences.s529-s5: He raised a Persian army; it outnumbered the enemy divisions by thirty to one.
sentences.s529-s6: One day, from morning until night they sprang upon them; the sovereign’s army fled.
sentences.s529-s7: Because so many were killed and so many were exhausted on the plain, the battlefield became cramped everywhere.
sentences.s529-s8: Except for the king and the low-born troops, no famed warriors were present on that battlefield.
sentences.s529-s25: When Ardashir's chief groom heard that, he took from the flock a few elderly guides.
sentences.s529-s26: The spahbed came down from the mountain to that village; from that village a light-footed man went ahead of him (a notable one).
sentences.s529-s27: He sent horsemen, young and old, from that city as far as Khoreh Ardashir.
sentences.s529-s28: When the army received word from the king, all, joyful of heart, took up the road.
sentences.s529-s1: A force from Istakhr took (or was under) Bi‑mar, and it was made ready so that it might do battle.
sentences.s529-s2: He sought a rightful reward from God by goodness, that it might pour down upon that soil and upon the thief's blood.
sentences.s529-s3: When King Ardashir entered the pass, he was welcomed, and Bi‑mar was made to go to war.
sentences.s529-s4: One deed of the glutton became difficult; he hesitated/refused, and the whole country became allied (or turned friendly).
sentences.s529-s9: From the blazing sun and the gusts of dust, tongues became chapped and cracked with thirst.
sentences.s529-s10: At that moment the night lifted a banner, that it might quiet that battle and tumult and commotion.
sentences.s529-s11: Someone saw a fire toward the mountain; the sovereign came with that company.
sentences.s529-s12: Radshir turned his face toward the fire; those same few men, young and old.
sentences.s529-s21: He asked that chief groom of the king's road, 'From here, where can I find the tomb?'
sentences.s529-s22: He answered thus: 'You will not find a settled (inhabited) place unless you have a guide.'
sentences.s529-s23: From here the road is four farsangs long; when you go (that distance), the tomb will come into view.
sentences.s529-s24: And from that time his movement continued from village to village; into one place called the famed 'Mah.'
sentences.s529-s17: From slumber, the Kayi, as was fitting, placed his bedding beside his pillow.
sentences.s529-s18: When dawn rose from the sea of water, the Shah of Iran rose from sleep.
sentences.s529-s19: The head-groom came to his bedside and said, 'May Pedram be yours day and night.'
sentences.s529-s20: What came to pass that this spot was your road? — That it was not worthy of your sleeping-quarters.
sentences.s529-s13: When night closed in the shepherds looked; thereupon the guards saw the ewes and goats.
sentences.s529-s14: He descended from the royal retinue and the army; his mouth was full of the dust of the battlefield.
sentences.s529-s15: Ardashir asked them for water; at once they carried (him) with water and whey (yogurt-whey).
sentences.s529-s16: He did not rest, and for a short while grazed on whatever he saw; the dark night raised its head over the sleepers.
